leveling_principles.description: In layered DFDs, the input and output data flows of a parent process must match the external input and output data flows of its child diagram (after ignoring minor auxiliary flows), so that the hierarchy is balanced and understandable.
leveling_principles.detection_hint: For each decomposed process, compare the set of incoming and outgoing flows on the parent with the set of flows that cross the child diagram boundary; the two sets should be equal.

leveling_principles.description: Decompose the system into as few intermediate layers as possible while keeping each layer understandable; too many layers make the model hard to read.
leveling_principles.detection_hint: Check that the number of DFD levels (0,1,2,...) is not excessive and that each level adds meaningful detail without unnecessary fragmentation.

leveling_principles.description: Within a single DFD, the granularity of processes should be roughly uniform so that some parts are not at basic-processing level while others still require several further layers of decomposition.
leveling_principles.detection_hint: Inspect each diagram to see whether most processes are either basic or decomposable to roughly the same depth; if some nodes are much deeper than others, reconsider the partitioning.

decomposition_rules.description: A single process should usually be decomposed into no more than about 7–8 child processes; more children suggest that the decomposition boundary should be reconsidered or an additional intermediate level introduced.

Apply a top-down, data-flow-driven decomposition: start from the 0-level DFD that defines the system boundary and main input/output flows, then successively decompose major processes into lower-level diagrams following the main data flows.
